full_response:
Let  

 A[1..n] , n ≥ 3, contains pairwise–distinct real numbers.  
 Δ(i) ≜ sign( A[i+1] – A[i] ) ∈ {+ , –}, 1 ≤ i ≤ n–1.

In a unimodal array the sequence of signs is  

 + + … + – – … – ,  

i.e. it is first constantly ‘+’, then (once) turns to ‘–’, and never changes
again.  
Put differently

 a unimodal array has exactly one “bend’’ : an index p (the peak) such that  
  Δ(p–1)=+ and Δ(p)=–.

Every other index 2 ≤ i ≤ n–1 whose two adjacent signs differ,

 Δ(i–1) ≠ Δ(i),                                                       (1)

is called a bad-index (a valley if Δ(i–1)=–,Δ(i)=+ or a second peak if
Δ(i–1)=+,Δ(i)=– with i≠p).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A counting lemma
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Lemma 1  
Let B be the number of bad-indices (1) in A.  
Removing one array element can destroy at most two bends, therefore at
least ⌈(B–1)/2⌉ elements have to be deleted to make the array unimodal.

Proof.  
Deleting position j removes the two adjacent edges
(j–1,j) and (j,j+1); hence it can eliminate the bends whose vertex is
j–1 or j (never more than two).  To get down to ≤1 remaining bend we
must therefore delete ≥(B–1)/2 elements. ∎


Corollary 2  
If A is ε-far from unimodal, B > 2εn.               (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
The tester
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Choose the sample size  

 s = ⌈ (8/ε) log n ⌉                                          (3)

and run the following algorithm:

Tester-Unimodal(A , ε)

 peakSeen ← false  
 repeat s times  
  i ← uniformly random from {2,…,n–1}  
  x₁ ← A[i–1];   x₂ ← A[i];   x₃ ← A[i+1]  
  case
   x₁ < x₂ < x₃ :                     // pattern  ↑↑
    continue
   x₁ > x₂ > x₃ :                     // pattern  ↓↓
    continue
   x₁ < x₂ > x₃ :                     // a peak
    if peakSeen then output NO and halt  
    peakSeen ← true
   x₁ > x₂ < x₃ :                     // a valley
    output NO and halt
 output YES

The algorithm asks exactly 3s = Θ((1/ε) log n) array queries and does
O((1/ε) log n) arithmetic operations.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Correctness
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  (Completeness)  
    In a genuinely unimodal array every triple of consecutive elements is
    either ↑↑, ↓↓ or a (single) peak.  
    The tester never rejects in these cases, hence it outputs YES with
    probability 1.

2.  (Soundness)  
    Assume A is ε-far from unimodal.  
    By (2) at least 2εn indices satisfy (1).
    Picking an index uniformly from {2,…,n–1} therefore hits a bad index
    with probability at least 2ε.  
    The tester rejects whenever it sees a valley or a second peak.  It
    may miss a bad index only if the whole sample avoids the set of bad
    indices:

        Pr[ tester accepts ] ≤ (1 – 2ε)^{s}  
                             ≤ exp( –2εs )                (by e^{x} ≥ 1+x)  

    With the choice (3)

        exp( –2εs ) ≤ exp( –16 log n ) = n^{ –16 } < ¼  (for n ≥ 2).

    Hence the tester outputs NO with probability at least 3/4.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Complexity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Queries   : 3s = Θ((1/ε) log n)  
• Arithmetic: Θ((1/ε) log n)  
• Space     : O(1)  

Since O((1/ε) log n) dominates the additive
O(log n) term, the overall running time is
O((1 + 1/ε) log n) as required.

\boxed{\text{The above tester makes }O\!\bigl((1+1/\varepsilon)\log n\bigr)
\text{ queries, always accepts every unimodal array, and rejects arrays
that are }\varepsilon\text{-far from unimodal with probability }\ge 3/4.}  

Confidence: 0.21
